adams_view_validation.description: For any eval that produces a .py file, run it through Adams View Python batch mode to check for errors. This is a programmatic check — do not rely on the LLM to perform this check.
adams_view_validation.pass_condition: Exit code 0 (no ERROR: lines in aview.log and no Python tracebacks)
notes: The script locates mdi.bat automatically (VS Code setting → ADAMS_LAUNCH_COMMAND → ADAMS_INSTALL_DIR → default path)
Adams View runs in the .py file's parent directory and produces aview.log there
The --log-output flag copies aview.log to the eval output directory for review
The aview.log should also be saved as an output file alongside the .py script for the reviewer to inspect

evals.prompt: Write an Adams Python script to create a simple pendulum. It should have a single rigid link of length 200 mm and mass 1 kg, pinned to the ground at its top end by a revolute joint, with gravity acting in the -Y direction. Release it from 45 degrees and simulate for 2 seconds.

rubric.model_created: Script calls Adams.models.create() to create and name the model
rubric.units_set: Adams.DefaultUnits is set (length mm, force newton, mass kg, time sec or equivalent)
rubric.part_created: m.Parts.createRigidBody() (or equivalent) is called to create a rigid body
rubric.mass_properties_set_directly: mass, ixx, iyy, izz assigned directly on the part object — not via execute_cmd or string commands
rubric.markers_created_on_parts: Markers.create() used on the part and/or ground — correct local locations
rubric.revolute_joint_created: m.Constraints.createRevolute() with i= and j= marker arguments
rubric.gravity_created: m.Forces.createGravity() called; y-component set to negative value (−9806.65 or −9806.65 for mm units, or −9.80665 for m units)
rubric.initial_angle_set: Part orientation set to 45° for the initial release
rubric.geometry_present: At least one geometry (sphere, cylinder, or block) added to the moving part
rubric.no_execute_cmd_for_setup: execute_cmd() is NOT used for part creation, mass properties, or joint creation — direct Python API is used throughout
rubric.dot_path_names_or_object_refs: Objects are referenced by Python variable (preferred) or full dot-path string — no abbreviated names
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: Python script runs in Adams View without ERROR: lines or Python tracebacks in aview.log (validated by scripts/run_adams_python.py)

evals.prompt: How do I create a translational spring-damper force between two parts in Adams Python? The spring should have stiffness 5000 N/mm and damping 100 N·s/mm, with a free length of 250 mm.

rubric.stiffness_5000: stiffness=5000 (or equivalent parameter name) is present
rubric.marker_args: Both i= and j= marker references are provided
rubric.explains_when_to_use: Explains that TranslationalSpringDamper is the appropriate type for a 1-DOF spring along a line of action

evals.prompt: Write an Adams Python script to build a four-bar linkage mechanism. It has three moving rigid links: a crank (100 mm long, 0.5 kg), a coupler (250 mm long, 0.5 kg), and a rocker (200 mm long, 0.5 kg). The crank is pinned to the ground at the origin; the rocker is pinned to the ground 300 mm along X. Connect the free ends of the crank and rocker with the coupler via revolute joints. Drive the crank at a constant 360 degrees per second. Include gravity in -Y and simulate for 2 seconds.

rubric.model_created: Script creates a model with Adams.models.create()
rubric.three_moving_parts: Three distinct rigid bodies are created (crank, coupler, rocker)
rubric.four_revolute_joints: Four revolute joints: crank-to-ground, crank-to-coupler, coupler-to-rocker, rocker-to-ground
rubric.motion_on_crank_joint: A JointMotion is created on the crank revolute joint
rubric.constant_360_deg_per_sec: Motion function is '360D * TIME' or '2 * PI * TIME' (or equivalent — 360°/s = 2π rad/s)
rubric.correct_marker_locations: Marker locations position the joint connections at the correct geometric endpoints (origin, 100mm, 300mm, etc.)
rubric.mass_properties_direct: mass, ixx, iyy, izz assigned on each part object — not via execute_cmd
rubric.geometry_present: Geometry (cylinder or block) added to each of the three moving parts
rubric.object_refs_preferred: Marker references passed as Python objects (variable references), not string dot-paths
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: Python script runs in Adams View without errors (validated by scripts/run_adams_python.py)

evals.prompt: Write an Adams Python script that creates a simple pendulum with a design variable controlling the link length. Use a design variable 'link_length' with a default value of 200 mm. The tip marker position and any relevant mass properties should update when the design variable value changes (use Adams.expression() to link them).

rubric.dv_created: m.DesignVariables.create() creates a design variable named 'link_length' (or similar) with value=200.0
rubric.expression_created: Adams.expression() (or Adams.expression() with dv.full_name) is used to build a parametric expression string
rubric.tip_marker_linked: Tip marker location Y (or Z) is set using the expression referencing the design variable
rubric.gravity_and_joint: Model has gravity, at least one part, and a revolute joint to ground
rubric.explains_expression_vs_direct: Comments or explanation that Adams.expression() wraps the string so the solver evaluates it at runtime — not a Python float
rubric.no_hardcoded_200: The tip marker position is NOT hardcoded to -200 directly; it references the DV expression

evals.prompt: Write an Adams Python script for a crank-slider (oscillating slider) mechanism. The crank radius is 50 mm, the connecting rod length is 200 mm, and the slider translates along the X axis. Drive the crank at 2 revolutions per second. Simulate 2 seconds.

rubric.three_moving_parts: Three parts: crank, connecting rod, slider
rubric.joints_correct: Crank-ground = Revolute; crank-rod = Spherical (or Revolute if in-plane only); rod-slider = Spherical/Revolute; slider-ground = Translational
rubric.motion_function: JointMotion function = '4.0 * PI * TIME' or equivalent (2 rev/s = 4π rad/s)
rubric.geometry_on_parts: Geometry added to each moving part
rubric.correct_marker_position: Crank big-end marker placed at the crank radius from the pivot
rubric.adams_view_no_errors: Python script runs in Adams View without errors

evals.prompt: I have an Adams Python script that creates a simple pendulum and runs a simulation. After the simulation, how do I: (1) create an object measure for the angular velocity of the link, and (2) access the simulation results to get the X-position time history of the link's CM marker?

rubric.object_measure_created: m.Measures.createObjectMeasure() (or Measures.ObjectMeasure) is used to create an angular velocity measure
rubric.correct_characteristic: Characteristic name like 'Angular Velocity' and component 'Z' (or equivalent) is used
rubric.analysis_results_accessed: analysis.results[part_name_or_fullname] is used to access the output channel
rubric.cm_position_x_retrieved: results[...]['CM Position']['X'].values (or equivalent path) returns the time-history array
rubric.no_execute_cmd_for_postproc: Post-processing uses the Python API — not execute_cmd('result set_value ...')

evals.prompt: I need to model a rubber mount with nonlinear X-stiffness in Adams Python. The X force-displacement table is: at −10 mm → −8000 N; −5 mm → −3000 N; 0 mm → 0 N; 5 mm → 3000 N; 10 mm → 8000 N. The Y/Z translational stiffness is linear at 5000 N/mm. Write a Python script that creates the spline and the force element. Use a GeneralForce for the X direction (with AKISPL) and a Bushing for Y/Z.

rubric.spline_created: m.DataElements.createSpline() creates the nonlinear table with correct x/y data
rubric.correct_spline_data: Spline x=[−10,−5,0,5,10] and y=[−8000,−3000,0,3000,8000] (or numerically equivalent)
rubric.general_force_with_akispl: m.Forces.createGeneralForce() uses AKISPL() referencing the spline in x_force_function
rubric.bushing_for_yz: m.Forces.createBushing() provides the Y/Z linear stiffness
rubric.correct_bushing_stiffness: Bushing translational stiffness list includes 5000 for the Y and Z DOF
rubric.marker_args_present: Both i_marker and j_marker are provided for all force elements
